findings.title: Headline data-driven optimal-K (S*) selection step has no implementation
findings.quote:
for i in range(len(top_k)):
    print("top_k: ", top_k[i])
    lasso_idx = selector_lasso.select_samples(top_k=top_k[i])
    data_ext_lasso["S"] = 0
    data_ext_lasso.loc[lasso_idx, "S"] = 1
findings.claim: main.py loops over a fixed grid of Top-K values and reports bias/std/MSE for each K, but no code selects the optimal K = argmin_k MSE(S_k).
findings.concern: A core contribution and method step ('We develop a data-driven approach to select the optimal subset ... based on the MSE', Sec. 6 Step 2 / eq. near line 425) is never executed; only per-K curves are produced, so the claimed automatic selection of S* cannot be reproduced.
findings.resolution: Authors: provide the script that computes K* = argmin over the per-K MSE (bv) and reports the resulting single estimate, or clarify that K was chosen manually from the figures.
findings.paper_ref: Section 6 (Step 2, MSE-minimising selection); Contributions bullet line 71-72
findings.reason: Lines 159-199 of application/main.py confirmed: the loop accumulates per-K bias/se/bv lists and writes one markdown table per K, but no argmin or post-loop K* selection is present anywhere in main.py.

findings.title: No requirements.txt / environment file; unlisted deps incl. scikit-optimize
findings.quote:
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV, LinearRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from skopt import BayesSearchCV
from skopt.space import Real
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPRegressor
findings.claim: The repo ships no requirements.txt / environment.yml / setup.py (confirmed by find), yet imports torch, sklearn, skopt (scikit-optimize), prettytable, scipy, tqdm, joblib, matplotlib, pandas, numpy at module top level.
findings.concern: The environment cannot be rebuilt from a pinned spec, and `import skopt` (a non-default package) is required at import time even though BayesSearchCV/Real are never used, so the modules will fail to import if scikit-optimize is absent.
findings.resolution: Authors: add a pinned requirements.txt (with versions of torch/sklearn/skopt etc.) and remove the unused skopt / MLPRegressor imports if not needed.
findings.reason: find confirms no requirements.txt/environment.yml/setup.py/pyproject.toml anywhere in the repo, and lines 1-6 of lasso_selector.py match exactly with top-level `from skopt import BayesSearchCV`.

findings.title: simulate_data.py uses pd.DataFrame but imports pandas only inside __main__
findings.claim: generate_rct_data/generate_external_data reference `pd` (lines 23, 45, 100, 123) but `import pandas as pd` appears only inside `if __name__ == "__main__"` (line 139); there is no top-level pandas import (verified by AST).
findings.concern: Running the file as a script works (the __main__ block imports pandas before calling the generators), but importing simulate_data as a module and calling the generators raises NameError: name 'pd' is not defined (reproduced in _audit_code/out/check_sim_reproduce.txt before a workaround).
findings.resolution: Move `import pandas as pd` to module top level.
findings.reason: grep confirms `import pandas as pd` appears only at line 139 (inside __main__); line 23 uses `pd.DataFrame` inside a module-level function, so importing the module and calling the generator raises NameError.

findings.title: example/main_example.py has an unresolvable import and a wrong-package class
findings.quote:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from example.generate_data import generate_demonstration_data
from lasso_selector import SelectiveBorrowingLasso
from influence_selector import Influenceselector
findings.claim: The Section-7 entrypoint imports `from example.generate_data import ...` (a package-qualified path that only resolves if run from the repo root with `example` as a package) and imports `SelectiveBorrowingLasso`/`Influenceselector` from bare module names, while the sibling files `example/lasso_selector.py` and `example/influence_selector.py` are the intended versions.
findings.concern: Depending on the working directory the script either fails to import `example.generate_data` or binds `SelectiveBorrowingLasso` to the application-folder class whose constructor requires a `dataset` argument and whose `select_samples` has a different signature than the call at line 25, so the illustrative example (Figure 1) is not runnable as shipped without adjusting the import paths.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the exact run command/working directory for the example, and make the imports consistent (use relative imports or run as a package).
findings.reason: Lines 1-4 of example/main_example.py match the quoted imports exactly; the package-qualified `from example.generate_data` and bare `from lasso_selector` are confirmed inconsistencies that prevent straightforward execution.

findings.title: Paper states PSID NO=123 but shipped PSID_control.txt has 128 rows
findings.quote: while the PSID dataset includes NO = 123 samples.
findings.claim: The paper (§8.1) states the external PSID control set has NO = 123 samples; the shipped dataset/NSW/PSID_control.txt contains 128 rows, and main.py's NSW top_k grid runs up to 128, consistent with 128 external samples.
findings.concern: The reported external-control sample size does not match the data actually used; minor, but the exact N used in the NSW experiment differs from the text.
findings.resolution: Authors: reconcile the PSID sample count (123 vs 128) in the text and code.
findings.reason: Paper text (line 504) confirms NO=123; PSID_control.txt has 127 lines (no header, so 127 data rows) and the NSW top_k grid in main.py goes up to 128 — the auditor's row count of '128' is off by one, but the core discrepancy between paper (123) and actual data (~127) is real and confirmed.

findings.title: Reported std and bias come from one dataset realization with no Monte-Carlo repetition
findings.quote:
bias = np.abs(tau_borrow - tau_exp)
variance = np.var(psi) / X.shape[0]
variance_1 = np.var(chi) / X.shape[0]
se_1 = np.sqrt(variance_1)

bv = bias ** 2 + variance
se = np.sqrt(variance)
findings.claim: The 'std' column of Table 3 is the analytic plug-in standard error sqrt(var(psi)/n) of a single AIPW point estimate, and 'bias' is |tau_borrow - tau_aipw| on a single simulated dataset (data CSVs generated once with seed 42; main.py has no outer replication loop, confirmed by grep).
findings.concern: Comparative claims that ˆτif 'has a smaller standard deviation' and 'smaller bias' than baselines rest on a single dataset draw with no Monte-Carlo averaging or repeated-seed variation, so the differences may not be stable and no sampling distribution of the estimators is estimated.
findings.resolution: Authors: report mean +/- std over many simulation replicates (multiple seeds) rather than the analytic SE on one realization, so the std/bias comparisons reflect genuine sampling variability.
findings.reason: Lines 54-60 of estimator.py confirmed: bias is a single absolute difference and se is analytic sqrt(var/n) on one dataset; the top_k loop in main.py is not a Monte-Carlo replication loop.

findings.title: NSW pipeline MinMax-scales treated, control, external (and Y) groups independently
findings.quote:
scaler_rct_treat = MinMaxScaler()
data_rct_treated[numeric_cols] = scaler_rct_treat.fit_transform(data_rct_treated[numeric_cols])
scaler_rct_control = MinMaxScaler()
data_rct_control[numeric_cols] = scaler_rct_control.fit_transform(data_rct_control[numeric_cols])
scaler_ext = MinMaxScaler()
data_ext[numeric_cols] = scaler_ext.fit_transform(data_ext[numeric_cols])

X = ["age", "education", "Black", "Hispanic", "married", "nodegree", "RE74", "RE75"]

X_ext = data_ext[X].values
y_ext = data_ext["Y"].values
findings.claim: For NSW, three separate MinMaxScalers are fit independently on the treated, RCT-control, and external groups (numeric_cols includes the outcome Y), so each group's covariates and outcome are mapped to its own [0,1] range.
findings.concern: The comparability bias b = |mu_ext(X) - mu_rct(X)| and the borrowed-outcome estimates are then computed across groups whose covariate/outcome scales differ; a model trained on RCT-scaled X applied to external-scaled X (and per-group-rescaled Y) mixes incompatible scales, which can distort both the selector ranking and the AIPW estimate on the NSW experiment.
findings.resolution: Authors: fit a single scaler on the pooled covariates (or none) and avoid scaling the outcome per group; confirm whether the NSW results change under a shared scaling.
findings.reason: Lines 41-46 of application/main.py confirmed: three independent MinMaxScalers are fit on treated, RCT-control, and external groups respectively, with numeric_cols including 'Y'.
